question: Test Your Knowledge: How are bulkheads different than baffles?
choices: Bulkheads divide tanks into separate compartments; baffles have holes to let liquid flow through and reduce forward-back surge
Baffles divide tanks; bulkheads allow liquid to flow freely
They are exactly the same thing
Bulkheads control side-to-side surge; baffles control only forward surge
explanation: Bulkheads divide tanks into smaller separate tanks; baffles have holes allowing liquid flow through and reduce forward-and-back surge, though side-to-side surge can still occur.

question: Test Your Knowledge: Should a tank vehicle take curves, on ramps, or off ramps at the posted speed limits?
choices: Yes, tank vehicles can take any curve at posted speeds
No, tank vehicles should take curves and ramps well below posted speeds
Only on wet roads should you slow down
Tank vehicles should exceed posted speeds on curves
explanation: Tank vehicles must take curves and on/off ramps well below posted speeds because the high center of gravity and liquid movement make them easy to roll over at speeds safe for other vehicles.

question: Test Your Knowledge: How are smooth bore tankers different to drive than those with baffles?
choices: They require less braking force
They have stronger forward-and-back surge because nothing inside slows the liquid flow; require extreme caution when starting and stopping
They are easier to control than baffled tanks
They have better traction on wet roads
explanation: Un-baffled (smooth bore) tanks have very strong forward-and-back liquid surge because nothing inside slows the liquid flow. Drivers must be extremely cautious, especially when starting and stopping.

question: Test Your Knowledge: What three things determine how much liquid you can load?
choices: Tank color, weather conditions, and driver preference
The amount liquid will expand in transit, the weight of the liquid, and legal weight limits
Time of day, road type, and vehicle age
Tanker size, pump speed, and hose length
explanation: Loading amount is determined by: how much the liquid will expand in transit, the weight of the liquid, and legal weight limits.

question: Test Your Knowledge: What is outage?
choices: The time a tank sits empty between loads
A power failure in the pump system
The room left in the tank because liquids expand as they warm; different liquids require different amounts
The distance from the tank outlet to the pump
explanation: Outage is the space you must leave unfilled in a tank because liquids expand as they warm. Since different liquids expand by different amounts, they require different amounts of outage.

question: Test Your Knowledge: How can you help control surge?
choices: Drive faster to stabilize the liquid
Keep steady pressure on brakes, don't release too soon, brake far in advance, and increase following distance
Pump the brakes repeatedly during stops
Reduce following distance to apply brakes sooner
explanation: To control surge: keep steady brake pressure, don't release too soon, brake far in advance of stops, and increase your following distance to avoid hard stops.

question: Test Your Knowledge: What two reasons make special care necessary when driving tank vehicles?
choices: The need for extra engine braking and lower tire pressure
High center of gravity and surge of the liquid in the tank
Low visibility and narrow axles
Weak braking systems and poor steering
explanation: The guide says tank vehicles require special care because of the high center of gravity and the surge of the liquid. Those two factors make them more prone to rollover and harder to control smoothly.
